## [00:00 - 03:03]

But basically, it's really easy to create audio products, very easy, very inexpensive. You can do interview products, you can do products where you're just speaking and recording that. Great, great products, you know, relatively high perceived value. You can make these products downloadable, you know, just downloadable audio. Doesn't happen too often these days. Mostly if you're going to go pure online, people are doing video these days. You can put them on CDs and ship them out to people. Of course, CDs are getting pretty old school these days. It's probably the most popular type of product being put out right now. In fact, you're watching a video product right now. There's a lot you can do with video that you can't do with audio. You can make it a lot more impactful. You can, I mean, people can really judge your personality quite a bit more when you're on video. You can demonstrate stuff on video. Video has higher perceived value. And of course, you can take video, and if you create video, you can also strip out the audio. So you can give video and audio to people. Sometimes it's not perfect. Like if you're giving a demonstration in video, then stripping out the audio and giving them audio generally isn't all that great. But a lot of times the audio can be very useful to have. ## [03:04 - 06:12]

So with video, you can offer online delivery where people can watch it online or download it from your website. You can put it on DVDs. And then there's two main types of video. There's what we call full motion video. And that's, you know, like where you're actually, it's like on TV, you know, you're actually seeing the picture, it's shot with a camera. And then there's screen capture. And basically screen capture is what this video is right now. In most of my products, I like to mix up both the full motion and the screen capture, because people, you remember, when you put out a product, it's your job to make it as consumable as possible. You want to help people, you want to make an impact on people. So you want them to consume the product. One of the ways that I think, or I know from experience, that you can make it more interesting, more consumable is to mix in full motion with screen capture. Doesn't mean you have to do it. I'm just saying, you know, that's probably the highest standard. Full motion is great for some things, great for demonstrations, great for allowing people to see you and connect with you. Screen capture can be quicker and easier. It can also be good for real dense stuff, like what we're talking about right here, I'm going to be going through a lot of dense information for you. So, you know, it makes it a lot easier to absorb when you can see it and see the words in addition to hearing me talk about it. So, you know, the editing with screen capture is usually a little bit easier. Some of the tools are easier. You don't have to worry about how you look on camera. It's generally easier to get the audio right. And there, I'm going to give you a hint right here. We'll get to this later on. But one of the most important things you can do with your video is make sure your video has good audio. People will put up with amazingly bad video. But if the audio is really bad, they will tune out almost immediately. So, you know, I spend a lot of time worrying about the audio in my video products. And we'll get to all of that in just a few minutes. You can also put out ebooks. These are typically PDFs. You know, in the olden days, they had these special proprietary ebook formats. So, you could lock them down and put a lot of security on. But people mostly found those are a pain in the neck. So, now 99% of the ebooks out there are PDFs. And so, ebooks are wonderful because, you know, you can write one fairly quickly. And bingo, you're done. You put it up. Someone can buy it from you, download it immediately. And also now, you know, even beyond PDFs, we've got, you know, the ebook delivery on iPads and Kindles electronic book readers is really growing rapidly. So, there's a lot of potential here. I haven't really been a big ebook seller, big ebook fan. I've done it. But not often. And because generally, the perceived value of ebooks is relatively low,

But, so I'm not a huge fan of books, because it's a low perceived value. One of the things you got to decide is to go trade published or self-published. So, trade publish is basically like, you know, if a random house or Wiley or something, something like that publishes your book, that's called trade published, or self-publish as you do it all yourself. So, let's take a quick look here at the advantages and or some of the characteristics. Trade published, basically, if you're going to go with random house or Wiley or something like that, you give up lots of control. In fact, standard author contract, you don't even control the title of your book. They actually control that. A lot of times, it'll be a discussion, but when push comes to shove, they are the ones that decide what the title is. They decide all kinds of other things. So, you give up lots of control. You give up some of your rights to your intellectual property. So, you have to be really careful in what kind of contract you sign. You rarely make any money. That's just the way it is. The typical contract, you might get 8% or, as a first-time author, maybe 8% of the selling price. But actually, it's 8% of the wholesale price, which is, you know, so a book that sells for 18 bucks, you know, you might end up getting, you know, a dollar or something off of each book that's sold for 18 bucks. And those are rough numbers, but they're not that far off. And even if you get an advance, say you get, you know, a $5,000 advance, that's an advance against the book sales. So, you don't even start to make money on the actual sales until the

## [09:16 - 11:48]

royalties exceed that initial advance. You basically, most authors, trade-published authors, very rarely do they make any money. You know, there's the big exceptions. You know, if you write the next Harry Potter or something, you can make, you know, a ton of money. But that's incredibly rare. You still have to do all the marketing. So, don't think that just because Wiley, you know, signed you that they're going to, you know, magically start selling your book, they're not. It just doesn't happen. There, you still have to be the one that's out there doing the marketing. Hard to believe, but that's the way it is. I know lots and lots of authors and they all say the same thing. The one good thing about being trade-published, it can do a lot for your positioning. You know, in terms of like getting media exposure, getting asked to speak, you know, to keynote and events, and stuff like that, it can really help your positioning when you're an author. I mean, it's better if you're an author who sells a bunch of books, but even just being an author, it can really help your positioning as an expert or a guru or whatever term you want to, you want to use, especially among the press. Like if you want to get the press to start calling you up to interview you, you know, that's a whole another science, whole another art form there, but it's a lot easier when you've got a book.
